scores.verdict: Best-engineered of the set: 3 draw calls, instanced rings + in-place point buffer + modular recycling, HDR white-hot cores via ACES fake-bloom (no RT cost) — bulletproof 60fps and genuinely hypnotic.
Rock-solid and alloc-free — one static plane scrolled in-shader, ~5 draw calls, DPR/delta clamped, clean audio wiring; the canonical synthwave look is flawless but the most expected/safe concept.

The premium showpiece — liquid-chrome iridescence, analytic normals, hand-rolled half-res bloom + ACES/CA/grain — but 9 snoise taps/vertex plus a 5-pass HDR-MSAA chain is the real 60fps risk on integrated GPUs; RedFormat DataTexture is a minor WebGL1 edge case.

Clean single-draw 30k GPU swarm, frame-rate-independent smoothing, zero hot-loop allocs; heavy additive fill is bounded and safe, but a lone breathing sphere reads simpler/less compositionally rich than the others (also ships as an HTML fragment, not a full doc).

summary: All four compile clean, cap DPR, clamp delta, wire the analyser correctly (mic = analysis-only, file = tap + destination), and run allocation-free hot loops — no crash risks, no cheap-looking output. Cyber-tunnel and synthwave-grid are the two to ship: both hold 60fps with near-zero margin (3-5 draw calls, instanced/static geometry, fake-bloom via tonemapping instead of render targets) while looking expensive and on-brand. Liquid-metal-orb is the strongest visual by a clear margin and the most sophisticated code, but its 9-simplex-taps-per-vertex displacement plus a full half-res bloom+MSAA-HDR pipeline is the one module with a real risk of dipping under 60fps on integrated GPUs, so it loses the top slot on fps discipline. Neon-particle-swarm is solid but the least visually differentiated (one breathing sphere) and ships as a bare HTML fragment.
